coredata.dc:title: Transition metal-modified armchair graphene network for high-performance hydrogen evolution reaction catalysts
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Sakr, Mahmoud A.S.
Saad, Mohamed A.
Teleb, Nahed H.
Abd-Elkader, Omar H.
Abdelsalam, Hazem
Zhang, Qinfang
coredata.dc:description: This study explores the structural, electronic, and catalytic properties of armchair hexagonal graphene networks (AHGN) modified with single transition metal (TM) atoms from Period V. Substitutions of Ag, Mo, Nb, Pd, Rh, Ru, Tc, Y, and Zr were investigated, revealing significant changes in bond lengths, bond angles, and dihedral angles. The binding energies indicate that TM substitutions do not drastically destabilize AHGN, suggesting potential applications requiring modified electronic properties. Electronic investigations showed that TM substitutions influence the partial density of states (PDOS) and energy gaps (ΔE), with Tc significantly reducing ΔE, indicating a shift towards metallic behavior. Mulliken charge analysis highlighted the electron density redistribution around TM atoms and edge carbons, enhancing electronic conductivity and chemical reactivity. Based on the results, AHGN-Rh-H, AHGN-Pd-H, and AHGN-Mo-H demonstrate the most efficient catalytic performance for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), with ΔG values comparable to platinum, underscoring their potential for practical hydrogen production applications. Conversely, materials like AHGN-Nb-H and AHGN-Zr-H exhibit higher ΔG values, indicating lower efficiency for HER. Global reactivity parameters (GRPs) analysis revealed AHGN-Tc with the most negative chemical potential (−4.010 eV) and highest electronegativity (4.010 eV), indicating strong electron-accepting and electron-attracting abilities. These findings underscore the potential of TM-substituted AHGN for advanced electronic applications and efficient hydrogen production.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Armchair hexagonal graphene networks (AHGN)
Transition metal substitution
Electronic properties
Binding energy
Mulliken charge analysis
Hydrogen evolution reaction (HER)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12274361, 62174016), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20211361), and College Natural Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province (20KJA430004). This work is supported also by Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2025R468), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 12274361 , 62174016 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20211361 ), and College Natural Science Research Project of Jiangsu Province ( 20KJA430004 ). This work is supported also by Researchers Supporting Project number ( RSP2024R468 ), King Saud University , Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a, b) shows the front and side views of AHGN's optimized construction. (c) In AHGN, Period V TM is utilized in place of H1.
(a-f) PDOS, and ∆E of AHGN, AHGN-Ag, AHGN-Mo, AHGN-Nb, AHGN-Pd, and AHGN-Rh. The dotted vertical line is the Fermi level.
(a-j) HOMO and LUMO representations of AHGN and its based materials.
Mulliken charge distribution for AHGN, AHGN-Ag, AHGN-Mo, and AHGN-Nb, red color is the negative site, green color is the positive site, and black color is the neutral site.

(a, b) The optimized structure for AHGN and AHGN-Ag following the H atom's addition, along with the sites that have been chosen and evaluated for hydrogen evolution reactions in AHGN and its based materials.
Some important quantum parameters like bond length (Å), dihedral, bond angles (degrees), and binding energy (BE) for AHGN and its based materials.
Mulliken charge (MC) of the transition metal (TM) for AHGN and its based materials.
Several significant GRPs of AHGN and its based materials.
The change in the free energy (∆G) for the hydrogen adsorption represents the HER.

head.ce:title: Transition metal-modified armchair graphene network for high-performance hydrogen evolution reaction catalysts

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study explores the structural, electronic, and catalytic properties of armchair hexagonal graphene networks (AHGN) modified with single transition metal (TM) atoms from Period V. Substitutions of Ag, Mo, Nb, Pd, Rh, Ru, Tc, Y, and Zr were investigated, revealing significant changes in bond lengths, bond angles, and dihedral angles. The binding energies indicate that TM substitutions do not drastically destabilize AHGN, suggesting potential applications requiring modified electronic properties. Electronic investigations showed that TM substitutions influence the partial density of states (PDOS) and energy gaps (ΔE), with Tc significantly reducing ΔE, indicating a shift towards metallic behavior. Mulliken charge analysis highlighted the electron density redistribution around TM atoms and edge carbons, enhancing electronic conductivity and chemical reactivity. Based on the results, AHGN-Rh-H, AHGN-Pd-H, and AHGN-Mo-H demonstrate the most efficient catalytic performance for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), with ΔG values comparable to platinum, underscoring their potential for practical hydrogen production applications. Conversely, materials like AHGN-Nb-H and AHGN-Zr-H exhibit higher ΔG values, indicating lower efficiency for HER. Global reactivity parameters (GRPs) analysis revealed AHGN-Tc with the most negative chemical potential (−4.010 eV) and highest electronegativity (4.010 eV), indicating strong electron-accepting and electron-attracting abilities. These findings underscore the potential of TM-substituted AHGN for advanced electronic applications and efficient hydrogen production.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Meeting global energy demands without severely impacting the climate is crucial as the world's power consumption is expected to double to 27 TW by 2050 [1]. Power production accounts for three-quarters of greenhouse gas emissions, 66 % of NOx, and most particulate matter emissions [2]. To prevent drastic climate changes, shifting to clean fuels with high conversion rates and low CO2 emissions is essential [3]. The hydrogen (H2) economy offers a solution to global energy demands and environmental crises [4]. H2 fuel is clean, non-toxic, sustainable, and has the highest gravimetric energy density (142 MJ/kg), making it an attractive energy source [5]. Although hydrogen constitutes 90 % of the universe's total mass, it is not available in its molecular form on Earth [6]. H2 can be produced via thermal [7], electrolytic [8], or photocatalytic [9] methods using conventional or non-conventional resources. Electrolysis of water using decarbonized sources efficiently produces ultra-pure hydrogen (>99.999 %) after water vapor removal [10]. Currently, only about 4 % of H2 production comes from electrolysis due to the large thermodynamic potential (1.23 V) required at ambient conditions [11]. Therefore, robust, low-cost, high-efficiency, and durable electrocatalysts are needed to overcome this potential barrier for sustainable H2 production. Electrocatalysts are categorized into Pt-based [12], transition metal (TM) based [13], and metal-free electrocatalysts [14]. Pt catalysts exhibit the highest exchange current density and negligible overpotential values, but their scarcity and high-cost limit their widespread use [15]. TM electrocatalysts like Co, Ni, and Al undergo corrosion, restricting their catalytic activities and requiring alloying for practical applications [16]. Due to these limitations, exploring metal-free catalysts for HER is essential [17].

Recent progress in synthesizing nanoscale materials has enabled the exploration of low-dimensional materials for their unique catalytic properties [18]. Studies on 2D nanomaterials have aimed to improve catalytic stability towards HER [18]. Carbon-based materials are effective alternatives for applications such as electrocatalysis, microwave absorption, and impedance matching [19–22]. Carbon-based nanomaterials, decorated with non-metals or metal-rich nanoparticles, exhibit excellent catalytic activity [23]. Combined carbon and nitrogen-based electrocatalysts, known for their non-corrosive nature, low cost, and unique properties, have been explored for improved HER performance [24]. Graphitic carbon nitride (gC3N4) structures have shown attractive electrocatalytic properties due to their electron-rich nature, H-bonding capabilities, and enhanced surface characteristics [25–29]. The previous study employed Density Functional Theory (DFT) to design 2D catalysts for hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) and oxygen evolution reaction (OER). Graphitic carbon nitride (α-CN) doped with phosphorus (P), silicon (Si), and boron (B) showed reduced band gaps to 3.78 eV, enhancing HER and OER activities by 88.33 % and 29.35 %, respectively [30]. Transition metal (TM) anchored 2D holey graphyne (HGY) catalysts, with cobalt (Co), iron (Fe), and chromium (Cr), achieved HER activity with Gibbs free energy values of 0.21, 0.14, and 0.05 eV, respectively [31]. Modifications of o-B2N2 monolayers through vacancies and carbon doping also significantly improved HER activity by up to 86.71 %, indicating their potential as electrocatalysts and photocatalysts [32]. Single atom catalysts (SACs) for electrochemical water splitting highlight their potential for enhancing hydrogen evolution reactions. Fe-containing SACs demonstrate excellent activity and stability, with a favorable ΔGH of −0.06, while transition metal-doped Zn12O12 nanocages, particularly Sc@Zn12O12, show low activation barriers for hydrogen dissociation, underscoring their efficiency for hydrogen production [33,34].

Quantum dots (QDs) have garnered interest for their size-dependent properties, charge transfer abilities, and surface characteristics [25,26,35]. QDs are potential competitors in fields such as energy conversion [36–38], optoelectronics [39], sensors [40–43], and photocatalysis [44], with extensive research on their HER electrocatalytic and photocatalytic activities [45]. Various QDs, including inorganic QDs like transition metal dichalcogenides QDs, TiO2 QDs, and CdS QDs, as well as carbon QDs (CQDs) with different morphologies, have been reported for high-performance HER [46–48]. Inorganic QDs are known for high efficiency in photoelectrochemical devices, but their use is limited due to the toxicity and scarcity of elements like Pb, Cd, Ag, and In [49]. CQDs, besides having comparable optical characteristics to inorganic QDs, possess low toxicity, eco-friendliness, affordability, and easily attainable synthetic pathways [50]. These properties make CQDs suitable for diverse applications like bioimaging, drug delivery, sensing, and catalysis [51].

We suggest synthesizing the Armchair Hexagonal Graphene Network (AHGN) using methods such as chemical vapor deposition (CVD), which allows precise control over the graphene's morphology and edge configurations, leading to the formation of armchair units connected by CC bonds [52]. Additionally, chemical functionalization, edge-selective doping, or defect engineering can be employed to link graphene units while preserving their electronic properties [53]. Other potential methods include solution-phase exfoliation [54] and self-assembly of graphene precursors [55]. Doping with heteroatoms or functional groups may further enhance the HER activity by introducing active sites, improving electron transfer, and increasing reactivity [56]. These synthesis approaches are expected to optimize the structural and catalytic properties of AHGN for hydrogen production technologies. This study investigates the effects of substituting Period V transition metals into AHGN. Structural and electronic analyses reveal changes in bond lengths, angles, dihedral angles, and binding energies due to TM substitution. TM substitution significantly impacts the electronic density, altering the partial density of states (PDOS) and energy gaps, which influence conductivity and potential applications. Mulliken charge analysis highlights electron transfer dynamics and the catalytic potential of these materials. The research emphasizes the good performance of Rh, Pd, and Mo-substituted AHGN in hydrogen evolution reactions (HER), showing significant promise for practical hydrogen production applications.

In this study, we utilized Gaussian 16 software [57] to perform density functional theory (DFT) [58] simulations on AHGN and its related materials, focusing on optimized structures, electronic characteristics, and potential applications in the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) [59]. To achieve a comprehensive understanding of the system, we employed the hybrid M06 functional [60,61], which has been extensively validated and shown to provide reliable insights into optimized structures and electronic properties [62–65]. The LANL2DZ basis set [66] was chosen for its balance between computational efficiency and accuracy [67,68]. To account for Van der Waals interactions, which are crucial for accurately describing the interactions between AHGN, its derivatives, and hydrogen atoms, we included Grimme's dispersion correction (gd3) with the M06 functional [69]. Additionally, to gain a deeper understanding of the electronic structure, we used the Multiwfn software to calculate the partial density of states (PDOS) [70]. We conducted comprehensive analyses of the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO), as well as Mulliken charge distributions [71]. These analyses provided detailed insights into the electronic states and properties of the studied materials, enhancing our understanding of their behavior and potential for HER applications. The Global reactivity (GR) parameters like dipole moment (μ), chemical potential (ρ), electronegativity (χ), and chemical hardness (η) were calculated using the HOMO energy (EH) and the LUMO (EL). These GR parameters are obtained from the following equations: ρ =
E H
+
E L
2
1
, χ = −
E H
+
E L
2
2
, and
η =
E L
−
E H
2
3
[72,73]. The BE was determined using the formula [74,75]: BE = (NCEC + NHEH + ExNx - Et)/ Nt, where NC, NH, Nx, and Nt denote the numbers of carbon, hydrogen, a single TM, and the total number of atoms, respectively. Similarly, EC, EH, Ex, and Et represent the total energies of carbon atoms, hydrogen atoms, a single TM, and the final compound, respectively. The change in free energy (ΔG) calculated using this formula [43]: ΔG = EH⁎-E⁎-EH, where EH⁎ is the total energy of the system, E⁎ is the energy of the catalyst, and EH is the energy of the hydrogen atom.

The analysis of Fig. 1 and Table 1 involves a detailed discussion of the structural changes and binding energy variations upon substituting a single transition metal atom from Period V into the armchair hexagonal graphene network (AHGN). The AHGN structure consists of three armchair hexagonal graphene units linked together through CC bonds, as illustrated in Fig. 1a. While each of these individual graphene units is inherently periodic, the focus of our work is on the behavior of the network formed by their combination, which introduces non-periodic characteristics [76–78]. We selected AHGN as a theoretical model to simulate carbon quantum dots due to its ability to effectively represent the key structural and electronic features of these materials, as supported by previous studies demonstrating the accuracy and practicality of graphene-based models for understanding carbon nanostructures. We selected Period V transition metal atoms for their unique catalytic properties and optimal balance of stability and performance in enhancing the HER on the AHGN. The transition metal atoms used for substitution are silver (Ag), molybdenum (Mo), niobium (Nb), palladium (Pd), rhodium (Rh), ruthenium (Ru), technetium (Tc), yttrium (Y), and zirconium (Zr). While Tc demonstrates promising theoretical catalytic properties, it is important to note that the practical use of Tc is limited due to its radioactive nature, safety concerns, and preparation challenges.

The structural analysis of AHGN reveals its optimized construction, featuring a central pore where a hydrogen atom (H1) is attached to a carbon atom. The arrangement is detailed in Fig. 1 (a) and (b), which show the front and side views of the AHGN structure. The side view reveals that the AHGN structure is non-planar, evident from the C2-C11-C12-C13 dihedral angle of 157.369°, indicating out-of-plane distortions. Fig. 1 (c) shows a single Period V transition metal atom (TM) replacing the H1 atom in the central pore of the AHGN, significantly altering the electronic and structural properties of the network. The H1 site was selected for transition metal decoration due to its central location within the porous structure formed by linking three armchair hexagonal graphene units, offering optimal accessibility and interaction potential for enhancing catalytic properties. Upon substitution with various TMs, the bond lengths C2-C3 and C11-C12 undergo notable changes. The original AHGN has bond lengths of 1.399 Å (C2-C3) and 1.483 Å (C11-C12). The C2-C3 bond length increases slightly for all TM substitutions, with the largest increase seen for AHGN-Tc (1.442 Å) and the smallest for AHGN-Ag (1.413 Å). The C11-C12 bond length also shows variation, with AHGN-Zr (1.459 Å) and AHGN-Nb (1.456 Å) having the shortest, and AHGN-Ag (1.481 Å) and AHGN-Pd (1.479 Å) the longest bond lengths. The bond angle C11-C2-C3 in the original AHGN is 122.191°. Upon TM substitution, this angle generally decreases, with the smallest value observed for AHGN-Tc (115.527°) and the largest for AHGN-Pd (120.596°). The dihedral angle C2-C11-C12-C13 in AHGN is 157.369°, indicating non-planarity. TM substitution affects this angle, leading to variations from 135.888° (AHGN-Y) to 163.904° (AHGN-Nb), reflecting changes in the three-dimensional structure.

The binding energy (BE) is an important indicator of the stability of the TM-substituted AHGN structures. The BE for the original AHGN is 6.950 eV. Most TM substitutions result in slight decreases in BE, with values ranging from 6.933 eV (AHGN-Ag) to 6.955 eV (AHGN-Tc). The variations are relatively minor, suggesting that the substitutions do not drastically destabilize the AHGN structure. The positive value of BE indicates that the system remains stable, as a higher binding energy typically reflects greater structural stability. In contrast to previous studies on biphenyl boroxine covalent organic framework (COF) and its derivatives, which reported BE values in the range of 6–10 eV [79], our current findings indicate that AHGN and its based materials exhibit comparable stability. To assess thermal stability, we conducted ab initio molecular dynamics simulations on AHGN finite networks before and after attaching metal atoms to the pore. These simulations utilized the atom-centered density matrix propagation (ADMP) molecular dynamics model [80,81]. Fig. S1 illustrates the minor fluctuations in potential energy and bond length observed during 16 femtosecond molecular dynamics simulations at 400 K. These results confirm the thermal stability of the system at this elevated temperature. In summary, the incorporation of Period V transition metal atoms into AHGN leads to noticeable changes in bond lengths, bond angles, and dihedral angles due to the larger size and different electronic properties of the TM atoms compared to hydrogen. The binding energies indicate that these substitutions do not drastically affect the stability of the AHGN, making these TM-substituted materials potentially useful for applications requiring modified electronic and structural properties.

The analysis of Fig. 2 (a-f) and Fig. S2 (a-d) explores the projected density of states (PDOS) and energy gaps (ΔE) for AHGN and its derivatives with various single transition metal (TM) substitutions. The ΔE is calculated using this formula: ΔE = El-EH where El is the energy of LUMO and EH is the energy of HOMO. For pure AHGN, the energy gap (ΔE) is 3.593 eV, indicating its semiconducting nature. The introduction of various transition metals (TMs) into the AHGN structure affects this energy gap differently, reflecting the role of each metal in modifying the electronic properties. Tc and Y cause significant reductions in the energy gap, with values of 1.699 eV and 1.952 eV, respectively. Tc's pronounced reduction is due to its ability to introduce deep electronic states near the Fermi level, which overlap significantly with the conduction band. This overlap increases the density of states around the band edges, leading to a more substantial narrowing of the band gap. Y also introduces states that significantly affect the band structure, resulting in a lower energy gap, though not as dramatically as Tc. Zr narrows the energy gap to 1.802 eV. Like Tc and Y, Zr introduces additional electronic states near the conduction band, contributing to a substantial reduction in the band gap. The extent of reduction is comparable to that caused by Tc and Y, indicating that Zr's electronic states have a strong influence on the band structure.

Mo and Nb reduce the energy gap to 2.268 eV and 2.107 eV, respectively. These metals introduce electronic states that lower the energy gap, but the effect is less pronounced than with Tc, Y, or Zr. Mo and Nb's electronic states partially overlap with the conduction band, resulting in a moderate reduction in the band gap. Rh and Ru reduce the energy gap to 2.933 eV and 2.925 eV, respectively. The reduction observed with these TMs is moderate, suggesting that their electronic states influence the band structure, but to a lesser extent compared to Tc, Y, Mo, and Nb. Rh and Ru introduce states near the conduction band, but these states do not significantly overlap with the band edges. Pd results in a smaller reduction of the energy gap to 3.285 eV. This relatively modest effect is likely due to Pd's electronic configuration, which introduces fewer or less impactful states near the Fermi level compared to other TMs. Overall, the varying effects of different TMs on the electronic structure of AHGN are attributed to the different ways these metals interact with the host material's electronic states. TMs like Tc and Y, which cause more substantial reductions in the energy gap, introduce new electronic states that are closely aligned with the conduction band, leading to significant narrowing of the band gap. In contrast, TMs such as Pd have a smaller impact on the band gap due to introducing fewer states or less effective states in reducing the band gap. This variation allows for tuning the electronic properties of AHGN for specific applications by selecting appropriate TMs.

The HOMO and LUMO distributions for AHGN and its derivatives with various transition metal (TM) substitutions are illustrated in Fig. 3 (a-j). In the base AHGN, the HOMO and LUMO are predominantly spread over a large number of carbon (C) atoms, reflecting the broad electronic distribution characteristic of the material's semiconducting nature. However, upon substituting different TMs, such as Ag, Mo, Nb, Pd, Rh, Ru, Tc, Y, and Zr, a noticeable shift in the density of the HOMO and LUMO is observed. Specifically, the high-density regions of the HOMO and LUMO transition towards the TM atoms and their neighboring C atoms. This shift indicates that the TMs are significantly influencing the electronic structure of AHGN, concentrating the electronic density around the TM centers and affecting the surrounding C atoms. This redistribution of the HOMO and LUMO densities highlights the impact of TM substitutions on the electronic properties of AHGN. The TM atoms introduce localized states that modify the electronic interactions within the material, which can influence its electronic behavior and potential applications. For instance, the concentration of electronic density around the TMs can enhance certain electronic properties or modify the material's conductivity, aligning with the observed changes in energy gaps and the overall electronic structure.

The Mulliken charge (MC) distribution for AHGN and its derivatives (AHGN-Ag, AHGN-Mo, and AHGN-Nb) is depicted in Fig. 4 (a-d). The distribution is color-coded to highlight negative sites in red, positive sites in green, and neutral sites in black. The red color, indicating negative sites with higher electron density, predominates on the edge C atoms, suggesting these as nucleophilic sites due to edge effects. The green color, representing positive sites with lower electron density, is primarily found on the TMs and the C linking to the edges, marking these as electrophilic sites. Black, indicating neutral sites, is mainly seen on the internal C atoms, reflecting a balanced electron density.

The MCs for various TMs in AHGN-based materials are provided in Table 2 . For AHGN-Ag, the charge is 0.220, indicating moderate interaction. AHGN-Mo shows a charge of 0.320, indicating strong interaction with graphene. AHGN-Nb exhibits a higher charge of 0.586, suggesting a stronger interaction and higher electron density. Other metals like Pd, Rh, and Ru have relatively lower charges (0.081, 0.077, and 0.097, respectively), indicating weaker interactions with the graphene structure. AHGN-Tc shows a charge of 0.200, suggesting moderate interaction. AHGN-Y and AHGN-Zr have higher charges (1.039 and 0.700, respectively), indicating substantial electron density due to strong interactions.

The positive MCs on the TMs indicate electron loss and transfer to the AHGN structure, increasing electron density on the graphene. This electron donation marks the TMs as electrophilic sites and the edge C atoms, which receive these electrons, as nucleophilic sites. This charge transfer enhances the electronic conductivity and chemical reactivity of the material, potentially improving its catalytic properties. The differing MCs reflect the varied electronic interactions between the TMs and the AHGN, influencing the overall properties and potential applications of these materials.

A series of compounds, denoted as AHGN, and its derivatives with different metals, and their corresponding global reactivity parameters (GRPs) are presented in Table 3 . These parameters, including chemical potential (ρ), electronegativity (χ), chemical hardness (η), and dipole moment (μ), are derived from the HOMO and LUMO energies of the molecules. All compounds exhibit negative chemical potentials, indicating their tendency to accept electrons. The magnitude of ρ varies among the compounds, with AHGN-Tc having the most negative value (−4.010 eV), suggesting a higher electron affinity compared to others. All compounds have positive electronegativity values, suggesting their tendency to attract electrons. The trend in χ mirrors that of ρ, with AHGN-Tc having the highest value (4.010 eV), indicating its strong electron-attracting ability. η values are positive for all compounds, implying a certain degree of resistance to charge transfer. AHGN-Tc shows the lowest hardness (0.849 eV), suggesting it is more reactive and prone to electron transfer compared to others. Dipole moments vary significantly among the compounds, indicating differences in charge distribution. AHGN-Y exhibits the highest dipole moment (5.222 D), suggesting a significant charge separation within the molecule. Compounds with lower hardness (η) values, such as AHGN-Tc, are generally more reactive. Compounds with higher dipole moments (μ), like AHGN-Y, are more polar and may exhibit different physical and chemical properties compared to those with lower dipole moments. Compounds with high electronegativity (χ) and low chemical potential (ρ) values, such as AHGN-Tc, are likely to accept electrons.

The optimized structures of AHGN and AHGN-Ag following the adsorption of one hydrogen (H) atom are presented, highlighting the selected sites for hydrogen evolution reactions (HER). The specific sites for these reactions in AHGN and its derivatives are depicted in Fig. 5 (a, and b). Fig. 5a shows site A located above a C atom for AHGN, while Fig. 5b displays site B above the single TM in AHGN-Ag, acting as a single atom catalyst (SAC). The H atom is initially positioned about 2 Å from both sites A and B before optimization. Thermal stability after hydrogen interaction was assessed using ab initio molecular dynamics simulations, as shown in Fig. S3. The results confirm that the system remains stable at an elevated temperature of 300 K, highlighting its resilience and suitability for hydrogen-related applications. The HER process involves key reactions: H2O + e + * → H⁎ + OH− (Volmer), H* + H2O + e− → H* + OH− (Heyrovsky mechanism) and H* + H* → 2* + H2 (Tafel mechanism), where * represents the catalyst and H* denotes the catalyst after hydrogen adsorption at selected sites. The focus is on optimizing the subsequent reaction to improve HER efficiency. The ideal free energy change should approach zero for optimal catalytic performance.

The change in free energy (ΔG) for hydrogen adsorption across various materials, reflecting their efficiency in the HER are provided in Table 4 . Materials with less favorable HER performance include AHGN-Ag-H (ΔG = −1.15 eV), AHGN-Nb-H (ΔG = 0.78 eV), and AHGN-Zr-H (ΔG = 0.87 eV). Materials exhibiting moderate HER performance are AHGN-H (ΔG = −0.47 eV), AHGN-Ru-H (ΔG = 0.49 eV), AHGN-Tc-H (ΔG = 0.30 eV), and AHGN-Y-H (ΔG = 0.58 eV). AHGN-Mo-H, AHGN-Pd-H, and AHGN-Rh-H demonstrate favorable HER performance with a ΔG of −0.19 eV, 0.15 eV, and − 0.11 eV respectively. For reference, Pt has an ΔG of −0.09 eV [82], 2D-αCN-B-doped of −0.61 eV [30], 2D holey graphyne of 0.400 eV [31], and 2D- o-B2N2 of −0.17 [32], indicating high efficiency for HER. The lower the absolute value of ΔG, the higher the catalytic efficiency for hydrogen evolution. In this context, AHGN-Rh-H (−0.11 eV), AHGN-Pd-H (0.15 eV), and AHGN-Mo-H (−0.19 eV) exhibit the lowest absolute ΔG values among the materials studied, making them highly effective single-atom catalysts (SACs) for HER. While their catalytic performance is comparable to Pt (−0.09 eV), they surpass other materials such as 2D-αCN-B-doped (−0.61 eV) [30], 2D holey graphyne (0.400 eV) [31], Cu@B12P12 [83], Zn@COF [84] and 2D-o-B2N2 (−0.17 eV) [32]. This indicates that Rh, Pd, and Mo-based catalysts on AHGN surfaces offer significant potential for efficient hydrogen evolution compared to other single transition metal catalysts. In summary, AHGN-Rh-H, AHGN-Pd-H, and AHGN-Mo-H stand out as the most efficient catalysts for HER, demonstrating superior performance comparable to the benchmarked Pt. The high catalytic activity of these materials underscores their potential for practical applications in hydrogen production. The high ΔG values for AHGN-Nb-H and AHGN-Zr-H suggest that these materials are less effective for HER, guiding future research towards optimizing or exploring alternative catalysts.

This research presents a detailed analysis of Armchair Hexagonal Graphene Networks (AHGN) modified with single Period V transition metal (TM) atoms, revealing significant impacts on both structural and electronic properties. The substitution of TMs results in variations in bond lengths, bond angles, and dihedral angles, yet the AHGN structure remains stable. Electronic studies show that these TMs introduce new electronic states near the Fermi level, modifying energy gaps and suggesting potential for tunable electronic properties. Mulliken charge analysis indicates substantial shifts in electron density, which enhance the material's reactivity and conductivity. Our findings identify AHGN-Rh-H, AHGN-Pd-H, and AHGN-Mo-H as effective catalysts for the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER), with AHGN-Mo-H standing out due to its lowest ΔG, indicating superior catalytic efficiency compared to Pt. Among the materials studied, AHGN-Mo-H exhibits the best performance for HER. Furthermore, analysis of global reactivity parameters underscores AHGN-Tc’s high electron affinity and strong electron-attracting ability, with the lowest chemical hardness (0.849 eV) reflecting high reactivity.

Mahmoud A.S. Sakr: Writing – original draft. Mohamed A. Saad: Investigation. Nahed H. Teleb: Data curation. Omar H. Abd-Elkader: Conceptualization. Hazem Abdelsalam: Validation, Methodology. Qinfang Zhang: Validation, Supervision.
Not applicable.
This article does not contain any studies involving animals performed by any of the authors.
This article does not contain any studies involving animals performed by any of the authors.
All authors mentioned in the manuscript have given consent for submission and subsequent publication of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have declared no conflict of interest.

This work is supported also by Researchers Supporting Project number (RSP2025R468), King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2024.111745.
